conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me first review the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for your patience. To help me assess your eligibility for Child Benefit, I need to ask you some questions about your circumstances.

First, do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you inquiring about?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I have one child. His name is Alex and he's 18 years old. I'm wondering if I can still claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. To determine if you can still claim Child Benefit for Alex, I need to know about his current education or training status.

Is Alex currently in approved education or training? For example, is he studying A-levels, doing a course at college, or undertaking approved training?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't studying at the moment. He finished school and decided not to go on to college or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. I have a few more questions to fully assess your situation.

When did Alex finish his education - do you remember approximately when he left school or stopped studying?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He finished up last summer, so it's been a while now. He didn't sign up with any careers service or anything like that afterwards - he just went straight into working.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I need to ask one more important question to complete my assessment:

How many hours per week is Alex working in his current job?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: He works full-time at a warehouse. I think it's around 30-something hours a week, something like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me also confirm a few final details:

1. Do you live in the UK?
2. Does Alex live with you, or are you contributing financially towards his upkeep?
3. Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - we're in Leeds actually. Alex lives at home with me, and no, he's not getting any benefits himself.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Based on the government rules, I need to inform you that you would not be eligible to claim Child Benefit for Alex in these circumstances.

Here's why: While Child Benefit can continue for children aged 16-19 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex has left education and is now working 30+ hours per week. According to the official guidance, you stop getting Child Benefit if your child "starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training."

Since Alex:
- Is 18 years old
- Finished education last summer
- Is not in approved education or training
- Works full-time (around 30+ hours per week, which exceeds the 24-hour threshold)

He does not meet the eligibility criteria for you to continue claiming Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'm sorry this isn't the outcome you were hoping for. If Alex's circumstances change in the future - for example, if he decides to return to approved education or training and reduces his working hours to less than 24 hours per week - you may be able to claim Child Benefit again at that time.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 18 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period; Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
circumstances: Child is 18 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child works 24+ hours/week and is not in approved education
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 18 years old and finished education last summer. While Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex is not currently studying or in approved training. Additionally, Alex is working full-time at approximately 30+ hours per week. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works more than 24 hours per week and is not in approved education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 18-year-old son Alex. While Alex lives at home and is not receiving benefits in his own right, he left education last summer and is now working full-time (30+ hours per week). Child Benefit eligibility ends when a child aged 16-19 works 24 or more hours per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is 18 years old and finished education last summer. While Child Benefit can continue for children under 20 if they remain in approved education or training, Alex is not currently studying or in approved training. Additionally, Alex is working full-time at approximately 30+ hours per week. According to the eligibility rules, Child Benefit stops when a child starts paid work for 24 hours or more a week and is no longer in approved education or training. Since Alex works more than 24 hours per week and is not in approved education or training, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with their 18-year-old son Alex. While Alex lives at home and is not receiving benefits in his own right, he left education last summer and is now working full-time (30+ hours per week). Child Benefit eligibility ends when a child aged 16-19 works 24 or more hours per week and is not in approved education or training. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
